speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is February 25, 1996. The survivor is Henry Greenblatt; the interviewer, Hilary Helstein; the city/state, Encino, California, United States of America; language, English. Today is February 25, 1996. My name is Hilary Helstein, and I'm interviewing Henry Greenblatt. We're conducting the interview in Encino, California, in the United States of America, in the English language.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, can you please tell me your full name, and if it was different at birth? And spell both ways, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was born Heniek, which in Yiddish they called Hyam. But they usually-- home, they called me Heniek, H-E-N-I-E-K, Grinblat. And it was G-R-I-N-B-L-A-T, one T. I was born in Warsaw. And I was born in Praga, which is a suburb of Warsaw. It's the-- I believe it's the east bank of the Vistula River. So I was born. As far back as I can remember-- I must've been about probably four years old. And I remember my father, his name was Szyja-- S-Z-Y-J-A, Grinblat. And my mother's name was Hinda, H-I-N-D-A. And I had a older sister, her name was Fala-- F-A-L-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: November 1, 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what is your name currently?

speaker: interviewee
text: Currently, it's Henry, which I adopted in the United States because nobody understands what Heniek means. And it's Henry Greenblatt, spelled G-R-E-E-N-B-L-A-T-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: Henry, tell me a little bit about your parents. You said you remember back when you were very young.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can remember my father was a factory worker. He was a blue-collar worker. And it seems like I was a real tough little boy. Because when he had to go to work, I had to get my chocolate. I had to get my hot chocolate before he was getting ready and packed his lunch. My mother, preparing his lunch, had to give me my chocolate. Otherwise, I was screaming bloody murder. So I remember that from way back. And I remember we lived in one-room apartment, which was probably about maybe 4, 4-- it was like 16 by 16 size. And we had a bathroom on the floor, with 20-some-odd other neighbors. And that was the only running water we had-- was in the hall. We lived in a three-story building, and I had-- well, getting back, you know, to my real childhood that I could remember-- so we slept in one large bed, my father, mother, my sister, and myself. How we managed, I don't really remember very well. But we lived in this one-room apartment. We had a, a nice-sized terrace that faced the street. And I spent a lot of hours playing outside. So I remember that part. I remember my father going to work in the morning and coming back. I remember he used to take me to shul. [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SOBBING] I remember, [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] remember my sister. We used to play. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The first real memory what I have of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was my birthday. I don't remember which birthday it was, but they made me a nice party. They even bought me a little ring with my initials on it. I remember we used to, with my sister, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] sometimes steal the goodies when my parents went away. We-- they used to, like-- we didn't have much of sweets, chocolate or sugar and all that. But I remember when they went, we used to dig in into the hidden places and take out some sugar and lick it up. And that was before I was able to go to school. Now, I remember the death of this Polish marshal that ruled Poland-- Marshal Pilsudski. I remember his funeral very well, which was in 1935. I believe it was in November. And I went with my father to see this funeral-- which whole Poland was all in mourning because of this marshal.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I distinctly remember that point. I don't know why. It just stuck in my memory, that particular thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now other things-- getting farther, I remember my family, my uncles, my grandparents on my mother's side. I remember we used to come to various functions, like parties and birthdays of my cousins. [CRYING] I remember, on my father's side-- my father comes from a different town, not from Warsaw, which is about 100 kilometers east of Warsaw, that took about two or three hours by train. I traveled with my father. And my grandmother lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it called, the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: The time was called Siedlce. It's S-I-E-D-L-E-- I have to look. It's C-E, I believe, Siedlce, if I can memorize it correctly to spelling.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I traveled there about once a year with my father to visit his mother and his sister, which lived with my grandmother. They-- my, my, my aunt, my father's sister, was a what they used to call her in quiet, an old maid. She was probably a, a, a lady in her 30s, as I can remember her. She was very nice-- [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very nice to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-- my grandmother, of course, she was-- she owned a, a flour mill. And all the peasants would come to mill their grains. And they would bring all the goodies, like fresh rolls and fresh bread. And I enjoyed it very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember those times that I traveled there. And I, I liked it very much. And I couldn't wait for the day that my father would say, well, let's go see my grandmother. [CRYING] So that's part of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Siedlce, at that point.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then getting back, of course, to Warsaw, to my home, I remember my first day of school. I remember [CRYING] they put me in school quite, quite premature, they said. That was kind of-- because of my birthday, I wasn't even six years old when they put me to school because the school year started in September. And I went into school, and I remember my first day I met the teacher and some children by registration. And the following day, I had to go to school. And I was scared, and I didn't really want to go. And I carried on the whole morning. I used to tell them, well, maybe I will go, maybe I wouldn't go. And that went through the whole morning until I finally got enough guts, I suppose, to give my hand to my mother. And she walked me because I was afraid to walk by myself to school. It was quite a distance from our house to school. It was-- the, the school was on Zabkowska in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Jewish-- what they call it a folkshul, a public Jewish school. It was one of the few schools sponsored, I believe, by the Bund organization, the workers-- the Jewish workers of Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was sponsored by them. And it was a Jewish school in the Polish language. But they also taught Jewish in school, as I can remember. And it was probably supported by the Polish government-- that's why maybe they gave it the Polish language as a main language.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember my first day when I finally got myself into the class. And of course, I got to like it. And I didn't cause any problems anymore to my-- to my parents. And I was a good boy. And I went to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't a very good pupil. I remember that there were certain subjects that I couldn't comprehend. I was very slow in math, and my sister would do the homework for me. She was four years older than I, and she was-- [CRYING] [SOBBING] she was very bright. And I used to force her to do the homework for me, especially math.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was the first year of school. Then I remember wintertime, we-- my father had made me a sled. [CRYING] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we just had fun in the snow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he didn't have much time from work. He worked very hard. And the time that he had free, he would take me, my sister, on the sled and pull us, just like a horse. And we had a good time. And then, of course, I had skates. And I remember playing on the ice and running around. And the only problem I had-- that the neighborhood that we lived was a Gentile neighborhood. There was-- most of-- not most of the kids, all my friends was-- were Gentile. They were anti-Semitic. They didn't know, actually-- I-- I-- as I-- as I can understand, the children didn't know much, but everybody hated a Jew. And they used to beat me up for sometimes no good reason. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember one time, we were playing-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were playing soldiers. And it was a game. I don't know exactly how you would describe it. It's like two rows of kids, staying-- facing each other, within a 15- or 20-foot distance-- maybe 30-foot distance. And one of us would have to run and cut through the row-- the line on the other side. If he cut through, he was the winner. And whoever was last in the row would be the loser from the two groups facing each other. And the street that we lived, it was a very quiet street. There wasn't that much traffic on it. Very seldom you saw an automobile passing. But there was some automobiles crossing. And also, on the Radzyminska Street that I lived, it was a, a small railroad. We used to call it the [NON-ENGLISH], that it was going to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't really recall exactly the destination of that railroad. It wasn't a full-sized track of a railroad. It was a narrower track that had, like, five or six cars. And the commuter train would be running. I believe it's Radzymin because the street was Radzyminska that we lived. And the town that this little train was going was Radzymin. That was the destination of this town. And that's why I remember. This little commuter train would run like once or twice a day alongside the street. And when getting back to this game, what I tried to bring out, we played across. And at one time, a car came out from around the corner. And it just so happened that one of the kids was still crossing the street playing, trying to cut into the row of the other group. And the kid was hit by a car. I don't know the speed the car was running. But it just happened that the kid wound up with a broken leg. Well, we just got some help. And I don't remember exactly-- the ambulance came, or whatever happened. But after everybody went home, they came knocking on our door, that I was responsible for pushing this kid under the automobile.

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] OK, Henry, please continue telling me about the-- how they came to your house after this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, they came knocking on my door, accusing me that I pushed the kid under the car, which was ridiculous. I didn't know. I was just scared, hell. But I know my father was really angry at them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How can they accuse a little kid that was just playing with other kids, taking another child and pushing him under the car? But nevertheless, he went down. And he talked it over with it some of the neighbors. And they left me alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I quite-- couldn't, couldn't understand. I know that they didn't like me because I was a Jew. And I did play with them very well. And I was actually a, a good player. I was never to the point that they would be disappointed of me. I tried to be as good as the other kids. But they did dislike me because I was Jewish. And this was one of the things that I started to, to feel that I'm really not one of them like I felt before. Anyway, this incident went by, and I remember times when I was coming out of school. Our school was just about-- across the street from a Polish public school. And it seemed like when we used to go out of school, they were waiting for us. And the kids-- not quite our age, because I was, at that time, maybe, in this second grade, already. And there were kids probably from the fourth, fifth grade. And they were just beating us up as much as they could, throwing rocks at us, and screaming, 'dirty Jew' at us, [POLISH], which is the right expression in Polish for telling somebody that you are a filthy Jew. And many times, I would run home and be chased by quite a few of them. I remember at one point-- at one time, I even have a scar until today on my foot-- on my-- on my thigh. I tried to run away from them, and there was a telephone pole. And I climbed up as much as I could. And they were on the bottom. They tried to catch me. I don't know if they were making fun of me, or they really tried to catch me. And I-- by climbing up, there was, like, a cable wire running alongside the pole that I ripped my thigh pretty bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there were quite a few incident that I was actually beaten up by them. But I always survived. And I had no other children to play. So these were my friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them were better to me, some of them were worse to me. And I used to come from school, and of course, do my homework. And then I went out in the street. And my mother always used to tell me, don't go out, don't go out. But I never listened. And I always snuck out. And I continued playing with them. So that's as far as my, my friends were concerned. I had a few Jewish friends in school, but they all seemed to live a little bit closer to the school. And I think I was about the-- oh, no, there was another boy that lived a little bit-- a little bit farther than I. He lived, I would say, about maybe a kilometer past my house. Occasionally, I would go to his place. His father was a, a caretaker of a-- of a lumberyard. And they used to have dogs over there. And I, I loved to play with dogs. So something pulled me over there to play with the dogs. And I remember once, one of the dogs even bit me. And my father tried to find out if the dog was sick or clean. But he couldn't get in, and they wouldn't tell him. So I don't know at that time what it meant. But he must've been worried that I had rabies or something because the dog took a nice chunk out of me. And luckily, everything went by. But I remember this other boy, that we used to play sometimes. But really, that was like a distance, and my mother didn't like me to go that far away from the house. So I tried to play, basically, around the house, near my house. And then I remember my sister had some friends, which they were older. She was born in 1926. She was four years older than I. And she had girlfriends that they sometimes formed a group, like a little reading group. To me, it seemed kind of funny, you know, girls sitting and reading poetry. I didn't like that. I didn't care. I always wanted to, to play kind of rough. But many times she would take me, if there was a birthday party for one of her friends. And I had a good time with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your Jewish life like at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father was a secular Jew. He was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] going to shul not every week. He was going to shul, like, on High Holidays. I remember we used to go to shul. And I don't remember him praying or anything, but he would attend shul. And I would go with him. The shul was in Praga, on the Szeroka Street. They also had a public bath over there because we didn't have any running water in the apartment. So we used to go there once a week to bathe. And I'd go with him. And I remember the times that we-- he wasn't really going to pray, but he was going to see his friends. And then, of course, we, we were showering over there. So we used to go there once a week. And I also remember the, the mikveh, which was a, a ritual place. It's like a little swimming pool. To me, it sounded like a swimming pool, because I didn't understand anything else but having fun-- and submerge in the water and try to swim and try to have fun. So I used to attend that. And getting back, my mother was a religious woman. She comes from a religious home because my grandfather, his job was-- he was a peddler. He was selling Jewish articles, Jewish artifacts-- mezuzahs and tefillin.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember him coming once in awhile to our home. And my-- and my mother would warn us that grandfather is coming. And we got to clean up. And we got to make the house look more, like, Jewish. So we kind of-- I kind of felt like things were done more Jewish for my grandfather than for my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my mother used to light candles [CRYING] [SOBBING] every Friday. [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, when she finished a prayer, [SOBBING] she, [CRYING] she would kiss everybody. And then we ate dinner. It was very pleasant. [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And once in awhile, we would gather in our house-- my family would come. And I remember, in particular, I had an uncle that passed away a few years ago in Israel, in Beer Sheva. And he was the youngest of my mother's brothers. She had a large family-- she comes from-- I believe there were about eight children in her family. And he was the youngest. So he kind of spent more time with me than any of my uncles. He would come sometimes on a bicycle and take me and drive around with me in the park. And occasionally, we would go to see my grandparents that lived on Zabkowska near Targowa-- I believe it was Zabkowska Number 2, in Praga. And they lived with my uncle, which was the, the youngest. His name, they used to call him [NON-ENGLISH], which is Wolf. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was-- at that time, when, when I was small, they had-- another one of the sisters was married and lived with my grandparents, which-- later on, they moved to a different area in Praga, which they called Nowa Praga, which is the new part of Praga, they called it. So I even went to see them over there. My uncle was a barber. And he worked in a shop. I don't know if he was part owner of the shop. But they, they had a little girl. My aunt, her name was the same as my sister's, Faigela-- Fela. She was also a, a, a very nice lady. She would play with me, occasionally. Her daughter was really small at that time. And I kind of visited most of my uncles. I had an uncle that lived on Panska. He was a painter. I had an uncle on Nowy Swiat, which had a store for shoes. I remember occasionally he would give me a pair of shoes because my father would go with me and promise me that I'm going to get new shoes from him. So occasionally I would go and see him in his store. He was very nice to us. And I had another uncle that lived on-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I forgot the name. It's in Praga near the-- near the market. There was a, a-- like a swap meet, a large swap meet. And I just can't recall the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he lived over there. And then they had a little stand on the swap meet. And they were selling all kinds of household goods.

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt is still alive today. She lives in, in, in Holon. She is in an old-age home. Which I-- when I'm in Israel, I visit her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Henry, when did things start to change for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the things started to change for me, actually, when I was getting older, before World War II.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to stop and go to a-- the next tape--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fine.
